Henri Nathansen (17 May 1868 – 6 December 1944) was a Danish writer and playwright. Born in Copenhagen, Nathansen was a son of Polish-Jewish immigrants. He worked as a librarian and a journalist before becoming a full-time writer. Nathansen was a prominent figure in Danish cultural and intellectual life in the early 20th century. He was one of the founders of the influential cultural magazine "Klingen" and was a member of the Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts. Nathansen's literary output included plays, novels, and essays, and he was known for his social criticism and engagement in humanist causes. Nathansen's best-known work, the play "Mandschauvinisten" (Male Chauvinists), was first performed in Copenhagen in 1909. The play was a satire on the bourgeois society of the time, focusing on the oppression of women. Nathansen's plays often dealt with feminist themes and were considered revolutionary for his time. In 1915, Nathansen published "En dansk krigsfange i Tyskland" (A Danish Prisoner of War in Germany), a memoir of his time in a German prison camp during World War I. The book was well-received and helped to establish Nathansen as a major literary voice. Nathansen was active in the resistance movement during the German occupation of Denmark in World War II. He was arrested by the Gestapo in 1944 and died in captivity shortly thereafter. Nathansen's legacy as a writer and cultural figure in Denmark is significant. His plays are still performed today and his social commentary on gender and class struggles remain relevant. Nathansen's life and work are a testament to his commitment to humanist values and his dedication to the cultural and intellectual life of Denmark.

Rufus Riddlesbarger was an American folk artist known for his colorful and whimsical paintings depicting rural life in the Appalachian Mountains. He was born in 1906 in Virginia and spent his early years farming and working as a carpenter before discovering his talent for painting in his 50s. Riddlesbarger's style was influenced by traditional Appalachian folk art, with vibrant colors, intricate details, and a childlike simplicity that captured the spirit of the region's culture and traditions. His paintings often featured scenes of farm life, including animals, crops, and rural landscapes, as well as religious and patriotic motifs. Despite his late start as an artist, Riddlesbarger's work quickly gained recognition and praise from critics and collectors. He was featured in numerous exhibitions and galleries across the United States, and his pieces were acquired by museums and private collectors alike. One of Riddlesbarger's most well-known works is his painting "The Holiness People," which depicts a congregation of worshippers in a rural church. The painting is notable for its bold and striking colors, as well as its vivid and emotive portrayal of religious devotion and community. Rufus Riddlesbarger passed away in 1982, leaving behind a legacy of stunning artwork that continues to inspire and captivate audiences today. His works remain highly sought after by collectors and art enthusiasts, and his unique style and vision continue to showcase the beauty and richness of Appalachian culture.

The Autonomous University of Madrid (UAM) is a public research university located in the Madrid metropolitan area. It was founded in 1968 as an experiment in academic freedom and self-governance by the Spanish government. UAM is renowned for its research excellence and innovative teaching methods. The university is divided into eight faculties, which include Arts and Humanities, Law and Social Sciences, and Medicine. UAM offers degree programs in a wide range of disciplines, including physics, engineering, psychology, and business administration. It also has a prestigious graduate school that offers PhD programs in various fields. One of the notable features of UAM is its commitment to interdisciplinary research. The university has established several research institutes that focus on cross-disciplinary research areas, such as neuroscience, environmental studies, and social and economic analysis. UAM also has a strong international presence and collaborates with universities and research institutions across the globe. In addition to its academic programs, UAM is also known for its cultural and social events. The university hosts a number of festivals, conferences, and exhibitions throughout the year. It also has a vibrant student life, with a variety of sports clubs, cultural associations, and student organizations. Overall, the Autonomous University of Madrid is a world-class institution that is committed to excellence in research, teaching, and community engagement. Its innovative programs and interdisciplinary approach make it a leading force in the academic world, attracting students and researchers from around the globe.

Aditya Kapadia is an Indian-American entrepreneur and software engineer. He is the co-founder and CEO of PockitShip, a technology-driven transportation and logistics company that delivers large items such as furniture, appliances, and exercise equipment. Early Life Kapadia was born in Mumbai, India, and moved to the United States at the age of 17. He attended Northern Virginia Community College and then transferred to Virginia Tech, where he earned a degree in Computer Science. Career After graduating from Virginia Tech, Kapadia worked at several technology companies such as Amazon, Microsoft, and Capital One. He then co-founded PockitShip in 2014, with the aim to make shipping bulky items hassle-free and affordable. PockitShip has since grown to a full-service transportation and logistics company that serves customers in the Washington D.C. metropolitan area and northern Virginia. The company has also expanded its services to include home delivery, relocation, and warehousing. Kapadia has been recognized for his entrepreneurial and engineering achievements. In 2017, he was named Entrepreneur of the Year by the Tysons Regional Chamber of Commerce, and PockitShip was listed as one of the Top 100 Startups to Watch in the DC Metro Area. Personal Life Kapadia currently lives in northern Virginia with his wife and children. Outside of work, he enjoys playing golf and traveling. Conclusion Aditya Kapadia's entrepreneurial spirit and passion for making shipping and logistics more accessible have led to the success of PockitShip. As the company continues to grow, Kapadia's contributions to the transportation industry will undoubtedly leave a lasting impact.

A Fool Who'll is a fictional character created by English playwright, William Shakespeare, in his play, "As You Like It." The character is a jester or a fool, and his name is derived from a remark made by the character Touchstone: "That is the way to make her scorn you still: to suit her, to study, to praise her, to give her humours and argue with her. Why, says she, "I'm like A Fool Who'll." Touchstone later interprets this to mean that the speaker is acting foolishly by trying to please someone who is not interested in them. A Fool Who'll is a comedic character, and his sole purpose in the play is to entertain the other characters and the audience. He is known for his witty remarks and jokes, which often come at the expense of other characters in the play. The character is also significant because of the commentary he provides on the other characters in the play. He is able to see through the façades that they put up and often makes astute observations about their true nature. For example, he notes that the character Jaques is a melancholic man who enjoys wallowing in his own sadness. Overall, A Fool Who'll is an important character in "As You Like It" because he provides comic relief and serves as a commentator on the other characters in the play. His name has also become a popular phrase in the English language, used to describe someone who is acting foolishly or trying too hard to please another person.

The Spokane–Coeur d'Alene combined statistical area is a metropolitan area in the Pacific Northwest region of the United States. It is composed of two metropolitan areas: the Spokane metropolitan area, centered on Spokane, Washington, and the Coeur d'Alene metropolitan area, centered on Coeur d'Alene, Idaho. The two areas are connected by Interstate 90 and are located approximately 30 miles apart. The Spokane metropolitan area has a population of around 573,000 people, while the Coeur d'Alene metropolitan area has a population of around 170,000 people. When combined, the two areas have a total population of over 743,000 people, making it the 101st largest combined statistical area in the United States. The economy of the Spokane–Coeur d'Alene area is heavily focused on services and retail, with a significant portion of the population working in healthcare, education, and leisure and hospitality. Manufacturing and natural resource extraction also play a role in the area's economy, particularly in Spokane. The Spokane–Coeur d'Alene area offers a variety of recreational opportunities, including skiing, hiking, camping, fishing, and boating. The area is home to a number of parks and conservation areas, including the Mount Spokane State Park, Spokane River Centennial Trail, and Coeur d'Alene National Forest. The Spokane–Coeur d'Alene combined statistical area is also home to a number of educational institutions, including Eastern Washington University, Spokane Community College, North Idaho College, and Gonzaga University. Overall, the Spokane–Coeur d'Alene combined statistical area offers residents and visitors a wide range of economic and recreational opportunities in the heart of the beautiful Pacific Northwest region.

Amyema is a genus of parasitic plants that belongs to the Loranthaceae family. It consists of around 120 species that are native to the South Pacific, Southeast Asia, Australia, and the eastern coast of Africa. The plant derives its name from the Greek word "amyein," which means to suck, which is a reference to their parasitic life cycle. The Amyema plant is a hemiparasitic plant, which means that it lives on trees but still performs photosynthesis through its leaves. The plant has thick, fleshy stems that attach to the branches of the host plant. The plant also has a root system, but it is reduced and may not reach the ground. Amyema plants have adapted to their parasitic lifestyle by developing specialized root-like structures called haustoria that penetrate the bark of the host plant and absorb water, nutrients, and minerals from the host plant. This adaptation helps the plant survive in a nutrient-poor environment and makes them highly successful parasites. The flowers of Amyema plants are small and tubular and are usually red, yellow, or orange in color. They are pollinated by insects such as bees and butterflies that are attracted to their bright colors and nectar. The fruit of Amyema plants is a small berry that contains one or two seeds. The Amyema plant has a long history of human use. In some cultures, it is used for medicinal purposes to treat various ailments such as fever, cough, and skin diseases. The plant has also been used as a traditional food source by some indigenous communities. In summary, Amyema plants are unique parasitic plants that have adapted to their environment by developing specialized root-like structures that penetrate the bark of their host plant. This adaptation helps the plant to survive in an otherwise nutrient-poor environment. They are commonly used for medicinal and food purposes by some cultures.

Sitok Srengenge is a renowned Indonesian poet and literary critic. He was born on May 15, 1950, in Sukoharjo, Central Java. He pursued his undergraduate degree in English at Gadjah Mada University and his graduate degrees in writing and literature from the University of Iowa. Sitok Srengenge is known for his unique style of poetry, which often combines humor, satire, and social criticism. His works explore a wide range of subjects, including politics, religion, sexuality, and everyday life. His writing is characterized by his use of local Javanese idioms and references, which add a distinct flavor to his poetry. Srengenge has published many works of poetry, including "The Colonel's Dream," "Die Zauberflöte," and "The Birds of the Dark Sky." His writing has been recognized both nationally and internationally, winning awards such as the Southeast Asian Writers Award and the S.E.A. Write Award. Srengenge is also a respected literary critic and essayist. He has written extensively on contemporary Indonesian literature, as well as on international literature in translation. His critical work has been published in various Indonesian literary journals and newspapers, including Kompas and Tempo. In addition to his literary pursuits, Srengenge is also a translator and editor. He has translated works by Samuel Beckett and William Faulkner into Indonesian and has edited several collections of Indonesian poetry. Sitok Srengenge is a prominent figure in Indonesian literary circles, known for his wit, insight, and innovative approach to poetry. His work has helped to shape the course of modern Indonesian literature and continues to inspire new generations of writers and readers alike.

Charlie Haas is a former professional wrestler who had a successful career in the World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE) and other promotions. Haas was born Charles Doyle Haas II on March 27, 1972, in Edmond, Oklahoma. He attended Seton Hall University, where he was a standout wrestler and earned two NCAA Division I championships. Haas began his professional wrestling career in 1996, wrestling for independent promotions such as Combat Zone Wrestling (CZW) and Jersey All Pro Wrestling (JAPW). He signed with WWE in 2002 and was assigned to Ohio Valley Wrestling (OVW), WWE's developmental territory. He was brought up to the main roster in 2003, where he formed a tag team with Shelton Benjamin, called "The World's Greatest Tag Team." Haas and Benjamin quickly became one of the most dominant tag teams in WWE history, winning the WWE Tag Team Championship twice, as well as the ROH World Tag Team Championship and the IWGP Tag Team Championship. They were known for their athleticism and technical wrestling skills. After Benjamin's departure from WWE in 2010, Haas continued on as a singles competitor, but was eventually released from his WWE contract in 2012. Haas then wrestled for Impact Wrestling and Ring of Honor (ROH), where he teamed with his brother, Russ, as The Haas Brothers. In 2013, Haas retired from professional wrestling to focus on his family and other business ventures. Outside of wrestling, Haas has also worked as a personal trainer and founded a fitness center, Custom Muscle, in Texas. He is married to former WWE wrestler Jackie Gayda and they have four children together. Overall, Charlie Haas had a successful career in professional wrestling, and his contributions to the sport will not be forgotten. His technical wrestling prowess and tag team success continue to inspire new generations of wrestlers.

Appley House is a historic mansion located on the Isle of Wight in England. It was originally built in 1702 for the Worsley family, one of the most prominent families on the island. The house has since undergone various renovations and additions, but the original structure and style have been preserved. The house was named after the apple orchards that once surrounded it. In the early 19th century, the house was purchased by Sir Richard Simeon, a member of parliament, who made significant additions and renovations to the property. He added a grand entrance with columns and a portico, a new wing with a ballroom and a chapel, and a stable block. He also commissioned notable architect John Nash to create a picturesque bridge and the lodges at the entrance to the estate. In the late 19th century, the house was purchased by William Whitaker, a wealthy banker. He made further modifications to the property, adding a new wing and a tower with a views over the Solent. The house remained in the Whitaker family until the mid-20th century, when it was sold to the Isle of Wight County Council. Today, Appley House is used as a residential care home for the elderly and disabled. The house is set within extensive grounds, which include a park and a beach. The park features many rare trees and shrubs, which were part of the original Worsley family gardens. The beach, known as Appley Beach, is a popular tourist destination and offers spectacular views of the Solent. The house and its grounds are open to the public and visitors are welcome to explore the gardens and the park. The estate also hosts various events throughout the year, such as music festivals and charity events. Appley House is a significant example of British architecture and history and remains an important landmark on the Isle of Wight.

Birlinn A Birlinn is a Scottish Gaelic term that refers to a traditional wooden vessel commonly used in the west coast of Scotland during the medieval period. The Birlinn played a crucial role in both trade and warfare in this region, serving as a means of transportation for goods such as livestock, timber, and food, as well as being used as a warship to defend against Viking raids. The name "Birlinn" comes from the Gaelic word "bàrlinn," which means "little ship." These small vessels were typically around 20 to 30 feet long, with one mast and one square sail. They were designed to be able to navigate the rugged coastal waters of Scotland and were equipped with a shallow draft to allow them to venture into shallow inlets and bays. Birlinns were constructed from local timber, usually oak planks over a frame of oak ribs and frames. They were also reinforced with iron nails and fittings to make them more durable and seaworthy. The typical Birlinn had a two-level deck, with the upper deck being used for crew quarters and the lower deck being used for storage. Birlinns were also fitted with a variety of weapons, including a cast bronze cannon known as a "leoch," which was used to defend against attacks from other ships. The vessels were also equipped with beaks or rostra, which were used to ram other ships during battles. The use of the Birlinn began to decline in the 16th century with the advent of new technologies such as muskets and artillery, which rendered wooden ships less effective in warfare. Today, the Birlinn is primarily used in recreations of historic naval battles or as tourist attractions in Scottish coastal regions. In recent years, there has been a renewed interest in the Birlinn as a symbol of Scotland's maritime heritage, and several groups have taken up the task of building replica vessels. These replicas serve as a testament to the ingenuity of Scotland's ancestors and the importance of the sea for the country's culture and history.

The Gulf of Aden is a strategic body of water that lies between the Arabian Peninsula and the Horn of Africa. It is connected to the Red Sea to the north and the Indian Ocean to the south. The gulf has a length of approximately 900 kilometers and a width of 550 kilometers, covering an area of approximately 230,000 square kilometers. The Gulf of Aden has been an important shipping route for centuries, connecting the Mediterranean Sea, Europe, and Asia. However, it became a hotbed of piracy in recent decades due to political instability, poverty, and little enforcement of maritime law. Piracy in the area reached its peak in 2011, with over 200 reported incidents in a single year. Several nations, such as the United States, China, and India, have deployed their naval forces to the region to counter piracy and ensure safe passage of commercial vessels. The international community has also implemented several measures to prevent piracy, such as increased patrols, cooperation between neighboring countries, and the use of private maritime security firms. Aside from piracy, the Gulf of Aden is also facing environmental challenges. The region is located in a hot and arid climate, which has led to overfishing, coral reef degradation, and coastal erosion. Climate change is also exacerbating these problems, with rising sea levels posing a threat to the low-lying areas surrounding the gulf. Despite these challenges, the Gulf of Aden remains an important economic and strategic location. It is a major transit route for oil exports from the Middle East, and is also a crucial gateway for trade between Asia and Africa. Protecting the stability and safety of the region is therefore crucial for the global economy and international security.

Apologeticus ( or Apologeticus) is a text attributed to Tertullian, consisting of apologetic and polemic. In this work Tertullian defends Christianity, demanding legal toleration and that Christians be treated as all other sects of the Roman Empire. It is in this treatise that one finds the sentence "Plures efficimur, quotiens metimur a vobis: semen est sanguis Christianorum." which has been liberally and apocryphally translated as "the blood of the martyrs is the seed of the Church" (Apologeticus, L.13). Alexander Souter translated this phrase as "We spring up in greater numbers the more we are mown down by you: the blood of the Christians is the seed of a new life," but even this takes liberties with the original text. "We multiply when you reap us. The blood of Christians is seed," is perhaps a more faithful, if less poetic, rendering. 

There is a similarity of content, if not of purpose, between this work and Tertullian's Ad nationes—published earlier in the same year—and it has been claimed that the latter is a finished draft of Apologeticus. There arises also the question of similarity to Minucius Felix's dialogue Octavius. Some paragraphs are shared by both texts; it is not known which predated the other.

Tertullian's brief De testimonio animae ("Concerning the Evidences of the Soul") is an appendix to the Apologeticus, intended to illustrate the meaning of the phrase testimonium animae naturaliter christianae in chapter  17).

Authorship
Not much is known about the life of Quintus Septimus Florens Tertullian. Some scholars believe him to have been a presbyter (priest) of the Christian Church, the son of a Roman centurion, and have him training to be a lawyer in Rome. Others, like David Wright, find that to be highly improbable. "No firm evidence places him in Rome at all, or for that matter anywhere outside of Carthage… It is in the well-educated circles in Carthage," Wright argues, "that Tertullian most securely belongs". Sometime after his conversion to the Christian faith, Tertullian left the Catholic Church in favor of the Montanist movement, which he remained a part of for at least 10–15 years of his active life and whose influence can be seen in many of his later works.

Ascribed date
Apologeticus, his most famous apologetic work, was written in Carthage in the summer or autumn of AD 197, during the reign of :Septimius Severus. Using this date, most scholars agree that Tertullian's conversion to Christianity occurred sometime before 197, possibly around 195. It was written before the edict of Septimius Severus (AD 202), and consequently, the laws to which Tertullian took exception were those under which the Christians of the 1st and 2nd centuries had been convicted.

Oldest extant manuscripts
"The present treatise depends on three authorities, none of them comprising the entire work. The first printed edition, by Martin Mesnart at Paris in 1545, containing chapters 1-19, was made from a manuscript now lost, but which seems to have been a copy of the already mutilated original of the eleventh-century codex Agobardinus (now at Paris). This, our oldest extant authority, contains a number of Tertullian's works, and of the present treatise chapters 1-30; in the first sentence of chapter 21 the copyist, following his original, which had lost a number of pages, passed on (without apparently noticing any discrepancy) to the middle of a sentence near the beginning of a different work On The Dress of Women."

"The study of the manuscripts of Tert. has established that in the Middle Ages several collection of works of this author were in existence1:
1. The collection of the Codex Agobardinus, the oldest extant manuscript of Tert.2.
2. The collection of the manuscript of Troyes 523 (Codex Trecensis) of the twelfth century3.
3. A collection represented by a number of manuscripts, which derive from a lost Codex Cluniacensis and a likewise lost manuscript from Hirsau (Württemberg), the Hirsaugiensis4.

Addressees and audience
This work is ostensibly addressed to the provincial governors of the Roman Empire, more specifically the magistrates of Carthage- "that the truth, being forbidden to defend itself publicly, may reach the ears of the rulers by the hidden path of letters"— and thus bears resemblance to the Greek apologues. It is structured as an appeal on behalf of the Christians and pleads "for toleration of Christianity, attacking pagan superstition, rebutting charges against Christian morality, and claiming that Christians are no danger to the State but useful citizens". Its readership is likely to have been composed of Christians, whose faith was reinforced through Tertullian's defense against rationalizations and rumours and who  "would have been hugely enheartened by Tertullian’s matchless confidence in the superiority of the Christian religion".

Genre
Apologeticus has the typical concerns of other apologetic works of his time, though it is presented in a much more complex manner. According to Wright, the text is constantly shifting "from the philosophical mode to the rhetorical and even juridical". Drawing from his training in literature and law, Tertullian demonstrates his talents as a Latinist and a rhetorician in an attempt to defend his newfound Christian faith. Tertullian's modern editor :Otto Bardenhewer further contends that Apologeticus is calm in tone, "a model of judicial discussion". Unlike previous apologists of Christianity, whose appeals for tolerance were made in the name of reason and humanity, Tertullian, influenced by his legal training, spoke as a jurist convinced of the injustice of the laws under which the Christians were persecuted.

Summary of Apologeticus
The following outline and summary is based on Robert D. Sider's translation of Apologeticus.

Introduction and addressing of unjust treatment of the Christians (Chapters 1–6)
The first section of Apology is concerned with the unjust treatment of the Christians, which Tertullian believes stems from the ignorance of the pagan populace. Simply put, he argues that people praise what they know and hate what they do not. To Tertullian this becomes evident in the cases of people who once hated because they were ignorant towards that they hated, and once their ignorance was gone, so was their hate. Their hatred prevents them from investigating more closely and acknowledging the goodness that is inherent in Christianity, and so they remain ignorant. And there is good in Christianity, Tertullian claims, despite the fact that people remain ignorant to it. Even when brought forth and accused, true Christians do not tremble with fear or deny their faith. It is the authorities that display bad behavior when they deny proper criminal treatment to the Christians. He argues that if Christians are to be treated as criminals, they should not be treated differently from ordinary criminals, who are free to answer to charges, to cross-question and defend themselves. In reality, the Christians are not free to say anything that will clear their name or ensure that the judge conducts a fair trial. If an individual says he is not a Christian, he is tortured until he says he is; if he admits to being a Christian, the authorities want to hear that he is not and torture him until he denies it. They resort to any means necessary to force them to either deny or confess, anything to acquit him. If all this done to someone simply for admitting to be a Christian, then they are surely making a mockery of Roman laws by basing all the charges on the name "Christian". Before hating the name, one must look at and study the founder and the school.

In addressing the charges, Tertullian plans to show the hypocrisy that surrounds these charges, demonstrating that those crimes exist among the pagan prosecutors as well. Then he analyzes the laws, claiming it suspicious that a law should refuse to be examined for error and worthless if it demands obedience without examination. If a law is found having an error and being unjust, should it not be reformed or even condemned? Faulty laws have no place in a just judicial system and should thus not be applied and observed. Here Tertullian mentions :Nero, and to a certain extent :Domitian, as examples of emperors who raged against the Christians through the use of unjust laws, simply for condemning "some magnificent good". He then brings up the good laws, and asks what has become of them; those that "restrained extravagance and bribery", "protected their [women's] modesty and sobriety", of the "conjugal happiness so fostered by high moral living that for nearly six hundred years after Rome was founded no sued for divorce". These traditions and laws are being ignored, neglected and destroyed and yet Rome chooses to concern itself with the "crimes" committed by the Christians.

Charges based on rumor answered (Chapters 7–9)
Tertullian begins by addressing the charges based on rumors, charges that vary from murdering and eating babies to committing incestuous and adulterous acts. Ultimately, he argues, they are simply rumors, for no evidence has ever been brought forth. No one has ever seen believers gather and supposedly commit impure acts or heard the cry of a crying baby because meetings and rituals are rarely performed in front of non-believers. It's all just lies and rumors meant to slander the Christian faith. Tertullian then makes the claim that Romans themselves are guilty of the very crimes it claims the Christians do. People from every age are sacrificed to Saturn and Jupiter all throughout the empire. The arenas are filled with the blood of those that fight, and the Romans even consume the animals that eat the bloodied bodies of the dead. For the Christians, murder is strictly forbidden; there is to be no killing or spilling of human blood, and that includes the killing the baby in the womb, for it would be destroying its soul. Neither are Christians allowed to eat meat that still has blood. 

Of the charges of incest and adultery, Tertullian says that Christians are not guilty of them, for they refrain from adultery and from fornicating before marriage, thus ensuring that they are safe from incest. Such behavior is different from that of the Romans, who through their immoral acts commit incest. This comes about simply through the case of mistaken identity: men go off and commit adultery, begetting children all throughout the empire who later unknowingly have intercourse with their own kin by mistake. In his attempt to make the Romans acknowledge their engagement in these acts, Tertullian hopes to demonstrate that Christians behave much differently from what they are accused of and that the charges should not hold.

Charges of more "manifest crimes" answered (Chapters 10–45)

Of the more "manifest crimes", as Tertullian refers to them, he first addresses the charges of sacrilege and says that Christians do not worship the pagan gods because the gods are not real, do not exist and thus hold no power or control over anything. Saturn, he claims, was once a mere man, as Roman lore and history will demonstrate. He then refutes further claims that the gods received their divinity through death, and wonders what business would the gods have with wanting ministers and assistants that are already dead. Such powerless beings cannot even be responsible for the rise and success of the Roman Empire. Furthermore, the character and nature of the gods themselves leave much to be desired; they are filled with rage, incestuous thoughts, envy, and jealousy. Why then should such imperfect and wicked beings be worthy of praise? He makes an even greater argument in referring to the pagan gods as demons, whose sole purpose is the subversion and destruction of mankind. They corrupt the souls of men by passions and lusts and rather successfully procuring "for themselves a proper diet of fumes and blood offered to their statues' images." In this way they draw attention to themselves and prevent people from turning to the true God. But even the demons acknowledge the power of God, and acknowledging this should be enough to clear the Christians of charges of sacrilege. For Tertullian, the Romans are at fault here for worshipping the wrong religion, not that of the one true God. But if the gods are real, and if the Christians are guilty of sacrilege, what does that say about Rome? The people all worship different gods and often treat their images with less respect than they deserve, using any opportunity to pawn their statues off and use them as sources of income. Tertullian further criticizes their literature, practices and ceremonies, calling them absurd and criticizing their philosophers’ disgraceful actions. Socrates would swear by dogs and Diogenes and Varro made less than respectful comments concerning the deities. The plays constantly make jokes about and mockery of the gods. Surely, he argues, the plays and the masks must be disrespectful to the gods. It is the Romans then that are guilty of sacrilege and impiety. 

Christians do not venerate these false and dead gods, nor do they treat them in such a casual and careless manner. They worship the One God, the Creator of the universe. Unlike the pagan gods, He is real and His very existence is proven by the testimony of the soul, which cries out "God" despite its weakened and fallen state. His works and the works of his prophets are preserved from Moses, who was 1000 years before the Trojan War and predates both Saturn and much of ancient Roman literature tradition, to many others key biblical figures. Tertullian goes on to briefly discuss the revelation of God through Christ. In order to do that, he speaks of the relationship between the Jewish people and God; they once enjoyed much favor from God but became "so filled with presumptuous confidence in their ancestors that they strayed from their teaching into manners of the world". Christ comes to re-establish the true doctrine; He is embodiment of Word and Reason, having been brought forth by God and thus having the title of Son of God. He adds, "his ray of God, then, was ever foretold in the past descended into a certain virgin and, formed as flesh in her womb, and was born man mingled (later changed to ‘united’) with God". Having given an explanation of the nature and divinity of Christ, Tertullian shifts to the charges of treason.  

To the charges that Christians do not offer sacrifice to Caesar, Tertullian says that it is practically useless to do that, for it is not in their power to give Caesar health, wealth and power. What they can offer to him they do through the use of prayer, because only God has absolute power and from him comes the emperor. He alone brings up empires and takes them down and only he is responsible for granting Caesar power, health and wealth; "We ask for them [emperors] a long life, undisturbed power, security at home, brave armies, a faithful Senate, an upright people, a peaceful world, and everything for which a man or a Caesar prays". Tertullian affirms that by praying for him, Christians are effectively putting Roman interests in God's hands as well as commending Caesar to God. In no way do their meetings endanger the state, nor do they involve plotting against the emperor, the senate, or the empire. Their treatment of the Roman Empire exhibits the same respect and well wishes that they display upon to their neighbor. Any other behavior would not be the sign of a good Christian.

Tertullian has addressed the charges and demonstrated that the charges faced by Christians are based on lies and rumors and that no such things have been committed. After all he has demonstrated, Tertullian marvels at how the pagan prosecutors still claim that being a Christian is a crime against the empire. Christianity in general presents no threat to public order and thus its members should be allowed to meet and live in peace.

Portrait of Christian society and of philosophy (Chapters 39–47)
Tertullian then proceeds to give an explanation of Christian life and practices. He describes the manner in which they come together to worship and please God; to pray for each other as well as for the emperor and the empire, to study and consider the Holy Scriptures, and to share food—but not before offering prayers and thanks to God. Afterwards everyone is free to share a song or something they learned from scripture, praising God all throughout the night. He continues by explaining the practice of tithing, the concept of loving one another and of being brothers and sisters, being united by their way of life under the teachings of Christ. And being part of this world, Christians have to interact with it and with others. They shop at meat markets and local shops, go to the baths and stay at inns just like everyone else, though in everything they do, they "bear in mind that we owe thanks to the Lord our God who created us". Despite this, the persecutors and accusers fail to recognize innocence when they see it, persecuting and treating the Christians unjustly. Christians know true innocence because they have learned and inherited it from God; they recognize and understand the eternal punishment that exists apart from God and acknowledge and fear the one who passes that real and true Judgment. Tertullian declares, "in a word, we fear God, not the proconsul". He also addresses the claim that Christianity is more than a philosophy, bringing up the philosophers that say, "it [Christianity] teaches the virtues and profess morality, justice, patience, moderation, and chastity". He makes the case that if Christianity is just another kind of philosophy, it should be treated the same way, granted with the freedom to teach and spread their beliefs and practice, customs and rituals.

Concluding considerations and remarks
Tertullian concludes his apology by likening the struggle of the Christians to a man fighting a battle. The Christians take no pleasure in being persecuted and enduring trials, but as soldiers of Christ they too must fight for the truth, all, of course, for the glory of God. Addressing the magistrates, he says "Crucify us- torture us- condemn us- destroy us! Your injustice is the proof of our innocence... When we are condemned by you, we are acquitted by God".

Karl Parker (born June 3, 1989) is an American professional rower known for his exceptional skill and success in the sport. Early Life and Education Karl was born to parents who were both rowers. At an early age, he developed a keen interest in the sport and began training under the guidance of his parents. He attended Brown University where he earned both an undergraduate and a graduate degree in economics. Career Karl’s career took off in 2008 when he competed in the under-23 world championships and helped the US team win a gold medal. He continued to excel in the sport and in 2012, he qualified for the US national team. In the same year, he competed in the London Olympics where his team took home a bronze medal. Following his success at the Olympics, Karl went on to compete in various world championships and won several medals. In 2014, he won a silver medal in the double sculls event at the World Championships in Amsterdam. The following year, he won a gold medal in the quadruple sculls event at the Pan American Games in Toronto. In 2016, Karl competed in the Rio Olympics where he and his team took home a silver medal. He retired from competitive rowing in 2018 after a successful career. Personal Life Karl is married to fellow rower, Emily Huelskamp, and the couple has a daughter together. When he is not rowing, Karl enjoys spending time with his family and participating in outdoor activities. Legacy Karl Parker is regarded as one of the most successful rowers in American history. He is a two-time Olympic medallist and has won several medals at world championships and other international competitions. His skill, dedication, and sportsmanship have inspired many young rowers to pursue a career in the sport.

Country Town is a 1971 Australian drama film directed by Peter Maxwell, produced by Fenton Rosewarne and starring Terry McDermott, Gary Gray and Lynette Curran. It was a film version of the Australian television series Bellbird, written by Barbara Vernon.

Plot
A severe drought strikes the town of Bellbird. Young reporter Philip Henderson arrives and stirs old tensions. The locals rally together and hold a fund-raising gymkhana.

Cast
 Fenton Rosewarne (Producer)

 Frank Rich  – Giorgio Lini

 Kurt Ludescher – Grossark

Production
The film was made in January 1971 during a break in production from filming the TV series. Although most of the regular cast were involved and the script was written by Barbara Vernon, who was one of the main writers on the show, the ABC was not formally involved in production. The movie was the idea of two regular cast members, Gary Gray and Terry McDermott who formed a production company, Avargo, with Fenton Rosewarne, an ABC film editor, and Rod Barnett, a chartered accountant. English director Peter Maxwell, who had extensive experience of working in Australia, was hired to direct.

The film was shot on 16mm over four weeks on a $70,000 budget starting late January 1971. Yea in Victoria was the main location with drought scenes shot in Wentworth, New South Wales. After editing was completed the Australian Film Development Corporation provided $15,000 to help prepare 35 mm prints.

Release
The film was distributed by Gray and McDermott themselves, released first in country areas then reaching Sydney in 1973.

Con Jones (August 18, 1869 – June 3, 1929) was an Australian born Canadian businessman and sports promoter, primarily active as a sports promoter in British Columbia during the 1910s and the 1920s.

He was inducted into the Canadian Lacrosse Hall of Fame in 1965.

Biography
Originally from the Woollahra area of Sydney in New South Wales, Jones arrived in Vancouver in November 1903 and first earned his living as a tobacconist.

Later on, during the 1910s and 1920s, Jones became one of the most prominent sports promoters in Western Canada, promoting field lacrosse and soccer in the Vancouver area. His teams played at the Con Jones Park (later renamed Callister Park) in Vancouver. In 1911 Jones made a big splash when he lured over lacrosse star Newsy Lalonde to sign with the Vancouver Lacrosse Club.

Jones died in Vancouver in 1929 and is buried at the Ocean View Burial Park in Burnaby, British Columbia.

José Diogo Dalot Teixeira (; born 18 March 1999), known as Diogo Dalot, is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a right-back for  club Manchester United and the Portugal national team.

Dalot is a product of the Porto youth system and made his professional debut for the club's  B team in January 2017. He made his first-team debut in a Taça de Portugal game in October 2017. After making eight appearances for Porto, he joined Manchester United in June 2018 for a reported fee of €22 million (£19 million). From October 2020 to June 2021, Dalot was loaned to Italian Serie A club Milan.

Dalot was a youth international and represented Portugal from under-15 to under-21 level. He was a member of the under-17 squad that won the 2016 UEFA European Under-17 Championship. He made his senior international debut for Portugal at UEFA Euro 2020.

Club career

Porto
Born in Braga, Dalot joined Porto's youth system in 2008, aged nine. On 28 January 2017, he made his senior debut with the B team, playing the full 90 minutes in a 2–1 home loss against Leixões for the LigaPro championship.

Dalot first appeared with the first-team in a competitive game on 13 October 2017, starting in a 6–0 away win over Lusitano de Évora for the season's Taça de Portugal. He first played in the Primeira Liga on 18 February 2018, coming on as a 75th-minute substitute in a 5–0 home routing of Rio Ave.

Manchester United
Dalot signed for Premier League club Manchester United on 6 June 2018 on a five-year contract for a fee of £19 million. Upon his arrival in Manchester, coach José Mourinho said that, considering his young age, he was one of the best right-backs around. He made his debut on 19 September 2018 in an away UEFA Champions League group stage match against Swiss side Young Boys, but was unable to have continuity in the team due to an injury sustained in the previous season. His debut in the Premier League happened on December 1 against Southampton in a 2–2 draw On 26 January 2020. Despite Mourinho's sacking and Ole Gunnar Solskjaer being appointed as the new manager, Dalot remained a valuable option: at the end of the season, he had had 23 appearances, 16 of which in the Premier League. During that season, he had a memorable match in Paris, in the Champions League quarter-finals 2 nd leg against PSG, where Manchester United completed a remarkable comeback thanks to a late penalty, won after a shot by Dalot. He scored the second goal in a 6–0 FA Cup win against Tranmere Rovers; it was his first goal for United.

During his first seasons for the club, Dalot struggled with various injuries and following the arrival of Aaron Wan-Bissaka, his performances were severely limited under manager Ole Gunnar Solskjaer.

Loan to AC Milan
Following sporadic use by Manchester United, Dalot was loaned to Italian Serie A side AC Milan for the 2020–21 season. He made his debut for Milan on 22 October, starting in a 3–1 win against Celtic in a UEFA Europa League group stage match. Seven days later, Dalot scored his first goal for Milan and provided an assist for compatriot Rafael Leão in a 3–0 home group stage victory in the UEFA Europa League against Sparta Prague. He made his Serie A debut on 1 November, replacing Davide Calabria in the 71st-minute of a 2–1 away win against Udinese. He made his first start in the league in a 2–2 away draw against Genoa. On 7 March 2021, Dalot score his first Serie A goa in a 2–0 away in at Hellas Verona. 

During the season, Dalot's versatility enabled him to play either as a right back and left back. This made him an integral part of Stefano Pioli's team, helping Milan secure second place in the 2020–21 Serie A and qualification for the 2021–22 UEFA Champions League after an eight-year absence. During his spell as a Rossonero, he was able to play regularly, making 33 appearances, scoring two goals and provinding three assists. As often said by Dalot himself, in Italy he was able to improve defensively, without losing his ability to attack.

Return to Manchester United

During the summer of 2021, Manchester United were interested in signing another right-back. Meanwhile, Milan, who were impressed with Dalot during his loan move with the club, began negotiations with Manchester United to sign him on permanent basis. After returning to Manchester United, he impressed coach Ole Gunnar Solskjaer with his performances during pre-season. Borussia Dortmund were also interested in signing him on a loan deal, but he decided to remain at United to compete with Aaron Wan-Bissaka for a starting position. On 22 September, Dalot was given his first start of the season, featuring in a 1–0 home loss to West Ham United in the third round of the EFL Cup. Since then, he has had limited opportunities with two starts and three substitute appearances. He played against Villarreal in a Champions League group stage match after Wan-Bissaka was suspended for two games. 

On 2 December, Dalot was given his first start in the league under interim manager Michael Carrick, putting an impressive performance and creating the second goal in a 3–2 home win over United's rivals Arsenal at Old Trafford. Following the arrival of interim manager Ralf Rangnick, Dalot cemented his place as starter for the club, following his solid performances in the club's next two matches against Crystal Palace and Norwich City.

International career

Youth
Dalot helped Portugal win the 2016 UEFA European Under-17 Championship, scoring twice in five games in Azerbaijan including once in the final against Spain. The same year, he helped the under-19 team reach the quarter-finals of the same competition.

With the under-19s, Dalot participated in the 2017 UEFA European Under-19 Championship, helping finishing as runner-up, after losing in the final to England. For his performances throughout the competition, he was named in the "Team of the Tournament". Dalot played for Portugal at the 2017 FIFA U-20 World Cup, starting in all the matches in an eventual quarter-final exit.

On 10 November 2017, he won his first cap for the Portugal under-21s, starting in a 1–1 away draw against Romania for the 2019 UEFA European Championship qualifiers. In March 2021, Dalot took part in the 2021 UEFA European Under-21 Championship. Portugal finished as runners-up after losing in the final 1–0 to Germany.

Senior
On 13 June 2021, Dalot was included in Portugal's squad for UEFA Euro 2020 as a replacement for João Cancelo, who withdrew after testing positive for COVID-19. He made his debut ten days later in the final group game – a 2–2 draw with France in Budapest – in which he replaced Nélson Semedo for the final 11 minutes. On  27 June, Dalot made his first start with the senior national team, in a 1–0 loss to Belgium in the round 16.

In October 2021, he was called up by Portugal and on October 9, he provided two assists, with the first being converted by Cristiano Ronaldo in a 3–0 home win against Qatar.

Style of play
Dalot is a physically strong defender known for his speed, technique and offensive capabilities. He can play as a full-back or winger on either flank, although he usually plays on the right. He is usually deployed as a wing-back on the right but in a more conventional full-back role on the left. As a left-back, he has been praised for his work ethic and defensive awareness. He has good dribbling skill and is noted for his involvement in counter-attacks by making crosses or long passes. His physique enables him to perform well in aerial duels.

Larry Wade (born November 12, 1969) is an American choreographer and creative director known for his work in the entertainment industry. He has worked with numerous high-profile artists such as Jennifer Lopez, Beyoncé, G-Eazy, and Katy Perry, among others. Early life and career Wade was born and raised in the San Francisco Bay Area of California. His passion for dance began at a young age, and he later went on to study dance and choreography at California State University, Long Beach. Wade's career started when he was hired as a lead dancer on Michael Jackson's Dangerous World Tour in 1993. This led to opportunities to work with other iconic artists such as Whitney Houston, Madonna, and Prince. Despite his early success, Wade never stopped learning and evolving as an artist. He went on to study acting, voice, and stage direction, which helped him become a sought-after choreographer and creative director in the entertainment industry. Career as a choreographer and creative director Wade's work as a choreographer and creative director has been seen by millions of people around the world. His choreography has been featured in music videos, live performances, and television shows. He has also worked on several major tours, including Beyoncé's "I Am... World Tour" and "The Formation World Tour." In addition to his work with individual artists, Wade has also contributed his talents to large-scale productions such as the Super Bowl Halftime Show, the MTV Video Music Awards, and the Academy Awards. Wade's style is known for its athleticism, precision, and creativity. He often combines elements of hip-hop, contemporary dance, and traditional African dance to create something completely unique for each project. Personal life Wade is married to fellow dancer and choreographer Karla Garcia. The two met while performing in the Broadway musical West Side Story and have been married since 2014. In his free time, Wade is an advocate for the arts and encourages young people to pursue their creative passions. He is also an avid traveler and enjoys exploring new cultures and experiencing different styles of dance. Conclusion Larry Wade's contributions to the entertainment industry cannot be overstated. He has worked with some of the biggest names in music and dance, and his choreography has been seen by millions around the world. His passion for the arts has inspired countless others to pursue their passions and continue pushing the boundaries of what is possible.

Līgatne is a small town located in the central part of Latvia, roughly 80 kilometers northeast of the country's capital city, Riga. The town is situated on the left bank of the Gauja River, which is the largest river in Latvia and a popular outdoor recreation destination. Līgatne has a long and rich history, with evidence of human settlement going back to the Stone Age. In the Middle Ages, the town was an important center of trade and crafts, and many of its historic buildings and structures still stand today. One of Līgatne's most notable attractions is the Līgatne Nature Trails, a vast network of walking paths and bridges that wind through the forested hills around the town. The trails offer visitors a chance to see local wildlife, including lynx, beavers, and a variety of bird species. Another popular draw is the Līgatne Paper Mill, a historic building that has been turned into a museum. The mill was originally built in the 19th century and was one of the largest paper mills in the Baltics. Today, visitors can tour the mill and learn about the process of making paper the traditional way. Līgatne is also a hub for outdoor sports and activities. The Gauja River is a favorite destination for kayaking and canoeing, while nearby forests offer opportunities for hiking and cross-country skiing. In recent years, Līgatne has become a popular tourist destination, attracting visitors from all over the world. Its combination of natural beauty, rich history, and outdoor recreation opportunities make it an ideal place to experience the best of Latvia.

Alhambra Theatre of Variety was a popular theatre located in Leicester Square, London, England. Established in 1854, it was known for its variety shows, music hall and vaudeville acts. The theatre was owned and operated by Oswald Stoll, a prominent British entrepreneur, from the early 1900s until its closure in 1936. The theatre had an impressive seating capacity of over 3,000 and was a popular venue for both locals and visitors to London. It hosted a variety of performances, including burlesque, comedy, drama, and musicals. Some of the leading performers of the time including Dan Leno, Marie Lloyd, and Charlie Chaplin showcased their talents at the Alhambra Theatre. In 1912, a fire broke out in the theatre causing extensive damage to the stage and auditorium. However, it was quickly rebuilt and reopened for business. During World War I, the theatre was used as a concert hall for soldiers and sailors. In the late 1920s, the theatre faced stiff competition from the growing popularity of cinema, which had become a more affordable form of entertainment for the masses. The Alhambra Theatre tried to adapt to the changing times by showing films and incorporating them into their entertainment program, but it was not enough to sustain the theatre's business. In 1936, the Alhambra Theatre of Variety closed its doors for the final time and the building was later demolished. Today, the Odeon Leicester Square cinema stands on the site of the former theatre. Despite its closure, Alhambra Theatre remains an important part of British theatre history. It represented a time when variety shows and vaudeville acts entertained audiences, and helped pave the way for the modern theatre industry.

Harvey armor refers to a type of armor that is named after its creator, Thomas Harvey, who was a renowned European armorer during the late 16th century. This armor was characterized by its unique construction methods, which included the use of overlapping plates of steel that formed a flexible and yet protective shell. This design allowed for greater mobility and flexibility in battle, traits that were appreciated by the warriors of the era. The Harvey armor is also known for its ornamental design elements, which often featured intricate engravings and etchings. Such elaborations were intended to reflect the status, wealth, and power of the owner, as well as to intimidate the enemy. Thomas Harvey, in particular, was known for his expertise in creating armor for royalty, aristocrats, and knights, and his works are highly prized in modern times by collectors and museums alike. The Harvey armor was primarily used during the Renaissance period and was popular among the European nobility, especially in Italy, France, and England. It was often worn during tournaments, jousting, and other outdoor events where protection and spectacle were both important. As firearms became more prevalent in the 17th century, the need for heavy plate armor declined, and the Harvey armor eventually fell out of fashion. Nevertheless, the Harvey armor remains a significant chapter in the history of armor-making, and its legacy can be seen in the evolution of armor design over the centuries. It has also inspired numerous reproductions and adaptations, which are still being made by modern craftsmen using traditional methods. Today, Harvey armor can be seen in art museums and collections all over the world, as a testament to the creativity, skill, and vision of a master armorer.

The Castillo de San José (Castle of San José) is an historic fortress and art museum in Arrecife on the Canary Island of Lanzarote.

History
Built between 1776 and 1779 the fort was constructed to provide a defensive stronghold in case of pirate attacks, and as a public works project to provide much needed employment during a time of famine and poverty on the island. It became commonly known as the Fortress of Hunger (Fortaleza del hambre). The famine was caused by a number of factors including periods of drought, and the earlier eruption of Timanfaya between 1730 and 1736, which devastated most of the productive agricultural areas on the island. Charles III of Spain, who was concerned for the plight of the islanders, ordered that the fortress be built.
 
Situated on a cliff above the Port of Naos, the D shaped fort has semi-circular walls facing the sea. On the landward side, the rear wall is protected by two small turrets, with a moat crossed by a lifting drawbridge leading to the main gateway. The fortress is constructed of masonry and ashlar blocks of volcanic origin. The interior which is made up of barrel vaulted rooms was mainly used as a powder store.

International Museum of Contemporary Art
In the 1970s the fortress was renovated and the interior redesigned by the artist César Manrique, who was born nearby, to house a museum of modern art.  The museum opened to the public in 1976 as the International Museum of Contemporary Art (, abbreviated as MIAC).

The exhibits are primarily from the period between 1950 and 1980, with a focus on various abstract art forms, such as modern sculpture, kinetic art, and geometric abstraction. The works are mainly by Spanish artists including Joan Miró,  Antoni Tàpies, Eusebio Sempere and the El Paso group, one section is also dedicated to the Canarian artist Pancho Lasso.

The courtyard is also used as a display space for outdoor sculptures and designs.
Beneath the museum, the annexe of the fort was renovated and converted into a restaurant, the two  connected by a spiral staircase. With curved floor to ceiling windows, the dining room has panoramic views overlooking the harbour and docks below.

The Seoul Korea Temple is the 37th operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church).

History
The first member of the church in South Korea was baptized in 1951. At that time Korea was in the midst of a war against Communist armies, with the UN intervening. Latter-day Saint servicemen from the United States were the first to bring the LDS Church's teachings to the area.

The first church's missionaries arrived in South Korea in 1954. Some years later, church apostle Boyd K. Packer was assigned to travel to South Korea and find a place in which to build a temple. After considering several locations, Packer eventually chose the property which the church had purchased almost two decades earlier. In 1981, the announcement was made for a temple in Seoul.

Gordon B. Hinckley, of the church's First Presidency, dedicated the Seoul Korea Temple on December 14, 1985. The temple's walls feature Korean granite with six white spires. A traditional, tiled "hundred-year roof" gives the temple a uniquely Korean appearance. Inside, the temple is decorated with delicate brush paintings, intricate wooden molding, silk wall coverings, gold leaf, dome chandeliers, and white lacquer furniture inlaid with mother of pearl.

After the temple was dedicated, a subway system was built in conjunction with the 1988 Summer Olympics in Seoul. The system included a line that ended right at the base of the hill upon which the temple was built, making the temple even more accessible for church members.

The temple is located near what is today Sinchon Station on the Seoul Subway Line 2. This station is located near four major South Korean universities:  Yonsei University, Hongik University, Ewha Womans University, and Sogang University.

The Seoul Korea Temple has a total of , four ordinance rooms, and three sealing rooms.

In 2020, like all the church's other temples, the Seoul Korea Temple was closed temporarily during the year in response to the coronavirus pandemic.

Presidents
Notable presidents of the temple include Spencer J. Palmer (1988–90) and Han In Sang (1996–2000). As of November 2019, the temple president is Chiwon Kim.

Tommie is a given name often used as a pet or nickname for Thomas, Thomasina, or Thompson. The name has origins in multiple languages including Scandinavian, German, and English. In Scandinavian languages, Tommie is a diminutive form of the name Thomas. Its meaning is derived from the Aramaic word "toma," which means "twin." Thomas was a biblical figure and one of the twelve apostles of Jesus. Its popularity spread throughout the Christian world after Saint Thomas was named as the patron saint of architects and builders. In the German language, Tommie is an anglicized spelling of the name Tommy. It is also a nickname for the name Tammo, which is a diminutive form of the name Theodor. Theodor means "gift of God" in Greek, and it was commonly given during the early Christian era to commemorate the idea that God had given his son as a gift to mankind. In the English language, Tommie is an alternative spelling of the name Tommy, which is a diminutive form of Thomas. In recent years, however, it has also emerged as a given name in its own right. Tommie has always been a popular name among celebrities and public figures. Some notable people named Tommie include the American football player Tommie Agee, the British footballer Tommie Hoban, and the American rapper Tommie Lee. Overall, Tommie is a versatile name that is both timeless and trendy. Its widespread use across different cultures and languages has made it a popular choice for parents looking for a unique yet familiar name for their child.

Round Oak railway station is a former railway station located on the Stourbridge Junction to Wolverhampton Low Level Line in England. It was opened in 1885 and served the area for nearly a century, closing in 1964 as part of closures to the rail network made by the British Railways Board. The station was located near the Round Oak Steel Works, which was at the time one of the largest in the country. The station had two platforms, both with basic wooden shelters. There was a signal box, a goods yard, and a small branch line from Round Oak which ran to the steelworks. The station was an important hub for workers of the steelworks and local residents. During the Second World War, the station and the surrounding areas were heavily bombed; the station was a target due to its connections with the steelworks and its crucial role in transporting materials and workers. Despite the attacks, the station continued to operate throughout the war, providing vital support to the war effort. After the war, as the steel industry began to decline, Round Oak railway station saw a decrease in passenger numbers, with many workers choosing to travel by bus or car. The station's small size and basic facilities meant that it was unable to compete with other nearby stations on the line which were larger and provided better amenities. In the early 1960s, as part of a sweeping programme of closures and cutbacks to the British rail network, Round Oak railway station was closed. The buildings were demolished, and the surrounding area was cleared for redevelopment. Today, little remains of the old station, but it remains a significant part of the industrial heritage of the area.

Con Jones was a well-known musician and composer, particularly celebrated for his contributions to the jazz scene in the United States of America. Jones was born on February 13, 1929, in New Orleans, Louisiana. Growing up in the heart of New Orleans, Jones was surrounded by a rich cultural heritage, which played a significant role in shaping his musical career. Jones began playing the trumpet at an early age, and by the time he was a teenager, he was already performing at local jazz clubs in New Orleans. Jones’ talent and hard work quickly caught the attention of many famous jazz musicians of that time, including Miles Davis and Louis Armstrong. In the late 1940s, Jones moved to New York City to pursue his music career. He quickly established himself as one of the most talented trumpet players in the city, and he began working with some of the biggest names in jazz, including Duke Ellington, Charlie Parker, and Dizzy Gillespie. Jones’ reputation as a composer began to grow in the 1950s, and he started writing and arranging his own music. Jones’ compositions soon became known for their unique rhythms and melodies, and he was widely praised for his contributions to the jazz genre. Throughout his career, Jones released numerous albums and performed in jazz festivals around the world. Jones was also a respected music educator, teaching at various universities and schools throughout his lifetime. Con Jones passed away on May 26, 2003, but his contributions to jazz music continue to be celebrated and remembered to this day. His talent and dedication to his craft have inspired many musicians and continue to inspire young artists today.

Hallgeir Pedersen (born 21 April 1945) is a Norwegian jazz guitarist and composer. He has been a prominent figure on the Norwegian jazz scene since the 1960s, known for his unique style and experimental approach to improvisation. Pedersen was born in Skien, Norway, and began playing guitar at a young age. He spent his early years playing rock and roll and blues, but later became interested in jazz after hearing recordings by Charlie Parker and Miles Davis. He studied at the Norwegian Academy of Music in Oslo, and began performing with some of the country’s leading jazz musicians, including Jan Garbarek and Terje Rypdal. In the 1970s, Pedersen formed his own trio, which included bassist Arild Andersen and drummer Jon Christensen. The group released several critically acclaimed albums, including "Briskeby Blues" (1974) and "Hallgeir Pedersen Trio" (1977). Pedersen also collaborated with other notable jazz musicians, such as pianist Chick Corea and saxophonist Joe Henderson. Throughout his career, Pedersen has been recognized as an innovator in jazz guitar playing, and has received numerous awards and honors for his contributions to the genre. He was named Jazz Musician of the Year by the Norwegian Jazz Forum in 1977, 1989, and 1995, and has also been awarded the Buddyprisen, the country’s highest jazz award. Despite suffering from Parkinson's disease, Pedersen continued to perform and record throughout the 2000s and 2010s. He released several albums with his trio, and also collaborated with other musicians on various projects. He passed away on 10 October 2021, leaving behind a rich legacy as one of the most important figures in Norwegian jazz history.

List of people with surname Johnson Johnson is a common surname of Scandinavian, English and Scottish origin. It is the second most common surname in the United States, after Smith. There are many notable people throughout history who share this surname. Here is a list of some of the most famous people with the surname Johnson: 1. Lyndon B. Johnson - The 36th President of the United States, serving from 1963 to 1969 2. Boris Johnson - Current Prime Minister of the United Kingdom, assuming office in 2019 3. Dwayne "The Rock" Johnson - Actor and former professional wrestler, who has starred in many blockbuster films like Jumanji and Fast and Furious 4. Magic Johnson - Former NBA player and co-owner of the Los Angeles Dodgers and Los Angeles Lakers 5. Samuel Johnson - A poet, essayist and literary critic who is best-known for his English Dictionary. 6. John Johnson - Founder of Ebony Magazine and Jet Magazine, which were important publications for African Americans during the civil rights movement. 7. Jack Johnson - The first African American heavyweight boxing champion 8. Amy Johnson - A pioneering female aviator who was the first woman to fly solo from England to Australia 9. Andrew Johnson - The 17th President of the United States, serving from 1865 to 1869 10. Robb Johnson- English acoustic singer songwriter whose career has been active since 1985. The list of people with the surname Johnson continues on and includes many more notable individuals across a variety of fields, including politics, entertainment, sports and more. It is worth noting that the Johnson surname has been a topic of intense interest in recent years due to the popularity of genetic genealogy testing, which has revealed connections between many people of the same last name who had no knowledge of one another. In conclusion, the Johnson surname has been carried by many influential figures throughout history. It is easy to understand why it is has become ubiquitous and popular throughout the world.

Catholics vs. Convicts is a 2016 documentary film about the 1988 Notre Dame-Miami football game directed by Patrick Creadon. It is the 87th film in ESPN's 30 for 30 series. The film was aired on ESPN directly after the 2016 Heisman Award Ceremony and premiered to over 2 million viewers, making it the highest rated 30 for 30 film since 2015.  At the 69th Primetime Emmy Awards it was one of that season's 30 for 30 films nominated for Outstanding Documentary or Nonfiction Series.

Background 
In 1985, a rivalry started between the University of Notre Dame and University of Miami football teams after the Miami Hurricanes beat the Notre Dame Fighting Irish 58–7. This loss marked head coach Gerry Faust's last game at Notre Dame.

The rivalry culminated in a showdown three years later, which was dubbed "Catholics vs. Convicts." The name originated from a t-shirt slogan created by Notre Dame students Joe Frederick and Michael Caponigro. The slogan played on Notre Dame's Catholic background and the public perception of Miami's players as college football's "bad boys," a reputation that was reinforced by the arrests of a number of Miami players. Both the Notre Dame Fighting Irish and the Miami Hurricanes came into the game undefeated. Cleveland Gary's controversial "phantom fumble" is examined closely in the film.  While studying the game the filmmakers re-examined a second close call by the referees which had gone largely unnoticed at the time.  Both coaches and several players from the game had never seen the replay, leading many of the subjects to reconsider the outcome of the game. Many sportswriters consider it one of the greatest college football games of all time.

The documentary is partially based on director Patrick Creadon's experience as a Notre Dame undergraduate at the time. Creadon was a senior at Notre Dame in 1988 and knew the students behind the "Catholics vs. Convicts" slogan.

Cast 
Creadon went back to interview several of his classmates for the documentary, including Patrick Walsh, his best friend from high school who was involved in the t-shirt scandal, and Tony Rice, the starting quarterback who lived down the hall from them in Dillon Hall. The film also features interviews with head coaches Lou Holtz and Jimmy Johnson; Notre Dame defensive coordinator Barry Alvarez; Notre Dame players Chris Zorich, Pat Eilers, Pat Terrell, and Todd Lyght, among others; and Miami players Steve Walsh, Cleveland Gary, and Leon Searcy, among others.

Reception 
Writing for The Wall Street Journal, John Anderson called Catholics vs. Convicts a "gripping gridiron drama." Sporting News ranks the film "among the best" of the 30 for 30 franchise.

References

Miami Hurricanes football
Notre Dame Fighting Irish football
30 for 30

Institutional dichotomy, according to John Wolfenden (responsible for the Wolfenden Report), in his essay The Gap — The Bridge, states that the dichotomization of intellectual disciplines by educational institutions, specifically collegiate institutions, is to blame for the communication gap between specialists in different fields.

Forced to pursue contrasting disciplines in college, students diverge from the broad educational background established in high school and pursue narrower studies. As a result, these students lose contact with the shared basis of their education as they venture into separate abstract studies. In effect, the communication gap of collegiate students widens as they become saturated with a curriculum of abstractions that relate to a single area of study.

For instance, a college freshman chooses mathematics as his field of study while another chooses English. As they study, the two students become grossly out of touch as they adapt to new languages which will soon serve as a code in their future careers. The mathematics student becomes consumed by symbols and numbers while the English student immerses himself in a sea of classical literary styles and grammatical mechanics. Consequently, the students soon become ill-equipped to communicate with each other. The mathematics student now becomes "illiterate" by standards of the English major and the English major becomes "innumerate" by standards of the mathematics major. 

Because knowledge is increasing, some fragmentation of disciplines is inevitable. The problem with fragmentation is that students are forced to live in ignorance of studies outside of their fields.

See also
The Two Cultures and the Scientific Revolution by C. P. Snow, 1959
